title: 顺水推舟
summary:
While discussing the nature of sin and luxury with Lynn Marchmont at Frognack, Hercule Poirot is interrupted by a frightened maid who reports that Rosalind cannot be woken and feels cold. Poirot rushes to Rosalind's bedroom and finds her lying on a four-poster bed. After checking her pulse, Poirot informs Lynn that Rosalind has been dead for some time and died in her sleep.

Near the bedside, Poirot discovers a small white box containing three remaining pills labeled "Take one before bedtime." On a nearby desk, he finds a note written in untidy handwriting in which Rosalind expresses feelings of wickedness and a need to find peace, ending the message abruptly with a dash. Rosalind's brother, David Hunter, rushes into the room and accuses Lynn and others of murdering Rosalind to obtain her money. When Poirot suggests the death may not be a murder, David points to Rosalind's healthy heart and her history of nervous problems as reasons to doubt a natural death.

Inspector Spence arrives and proposes an alternative theory: that Rosalind may have killed a man named Yadon in a fit of madness while in the Wormsley Valley, rather than being in London as David had claimed. During a subsequent discussion at the police station, Spence admits he had focused on David's alibi while overlooking Rosalind's. He suggests Rosalind might have attacked Yadon in a rage, noting her recent dazed behavior. Poirot clarifies that the pills found by Rosalind's bed were merely harmless bromide used for calming nerves.
